claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. The first thing I need to know is:

**How old is your child?**

This will help me determine the right path for assessing eligibility:
- If they're **under 16**, they generally qualify automatically (with some additional checks)
- If they're **16 to 19**, we'll need to check their education or training status
- If they're **20 or over**, they typically won't qualify

Please tell me your child's age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. My child Alex is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you! Since Alex is 8 years old, they fall into the under-16 category. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next check:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, I expect they're not working at all, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 8, so they're just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's what I expected. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

Again, since Alex is 8 years old and in primary school, I expect they're not claiming benefits in their own name, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, they wouldn't be in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm this. Apprenticeships typically start at age 16 or older.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - they're far too young for that!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You'll meet these requirements if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, OR
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months), OR
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days), OR
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family (and not claiming certain benefits), OR
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources, OR
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside, OR
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Which of these applies to you?** Most commonly, people simply normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - we live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That satisfies the residency requirement. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex**, OR
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents), OR
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do any of these apply to you?** Most commonly, parents live with their child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time. This is particularly relevant in situations where parents are separated or if other family members might be claiming.

- If **no one else is claiming**, we'll continue with the assessment
- If **someone else is claiming but you've agreed they'll transfer it to you**, we can proceed
- If **there's a dispute** about who should claim, HMRC will need to make the decision

What's your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following best describes where Alex is currently living?**

- **In hospital** (for an extended period)
- **In care** (looked after by the local authority)
- **Fostered** (living with foster carers)
- **Living normally** at home with you

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex has been in care for a couple of months, but they come home to stay with me on the weekends.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Alex is in care, I need to check the specific details.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. This is an important detail. Let me ask you:

**Has Alex been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR does Alex spend at least 24 hours a week at your home?**

You mentioned Alex comes home on weekends. Let me clarify:
- If Alex has been in care for **8 weeks or less**, you can still claim
- **OR** if Alex regularly spends **24 hours or more per week** with you at home, you can continue to claim regardless of how long they've been in care

You said it's been "a couple of months" - is that more than 8 weeks? And when Alex comes home on weekends, do they spend at least 24 hours with you each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been exactly 8 weeks, so right at the limit. And yes, when Alex comes home on weekends, they spend more than 24 hours with me each week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex spends at least 24 hours per week at home with you, you can continue to claim Child Benefit even though they're in care. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now for an important question related to Alex being in care:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council paying for Alex's care placement, or are you paying for it yourself? Generally, you cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is covering the costs of the child's accommodation or maintenance.

What's the situation with Alex's care arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: The local council is paying for Alex's care placement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me process this outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that based on the assessment, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

**The reason:** The local council is paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance while they're in care.

**Important information:**
You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. This applies to care arrangements where the local authority is funding the placement, even if the child spends time at home with you on weekends.

**What this means:**
- Even though Alex spends 24+ hours per week with you at home and you maintain a close relationship, the fact that the council is funding the care placement means Child Benefit cannot be claimed during this period.

**Possible next steps:**
- If Alex's care arrangement changes in the future and they return home full-time (with the council no longer paying for their accommodation), you would be able to claim Child Benefit at that point.
- If you have questions about your specific situation or think there may be exceptional circumstances, you can contact HMRC's Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - all of which would normally qualify them. The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester) and is responsible for Alex (living with them on weekends). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, Alex has been in care for 8 weeks with the local council paying for their accommodation and maintenance. Although Alex spends more than 24 hours per week at home on weekends (which would normally allow continued Child Benefit claims during care), the rule states that Child Benefit cannot be claimed when the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, Alex does not qualify for Child Benefit under these circumstances.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all personal eligibility requirements (UK residency, responsibility for the child, no competing claims). Alex meets the basic qualifying child criteria (age, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship). However, the determining factor is that the local council is paying for Alex's care placement. Under UK Child Benefit rules, when a local authority pays towards a child's accommodation or maintenance while in care, Child Benefit cannot be claimed, even if the child regularly spends substantial time at home with the claimant.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.reason: Local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance
response.guidance: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. This applies to informal care arrangements and some fostering situations.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - all of which would normally qualify them. The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester) and is responsible for Alex (living with them on weekends). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, Alex has been in care for 8 weeks with the local council paying for their accommodation and maintenance. Although Alex spends more than 24 hours per week at home on weekends (which would normally allow continued Child Benefit claims during care), the rule states that Child Benefit cannot be claimed when the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, Alex does not qualify for Child Benefit under these circumstances.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all personal eligibility requirements (UK residency, responsibility for the child, no competing claims). Alex meets the basic qualifying child criteria (age, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship). However, the determining factor is that the local council is paying for Alex's care placement. Under UK Child Benefit rules, when a local authority pays towards a child's accommodation or maintenance while in care, Child Benefit cannot be claimed, even if the child regularly spends substantial time at home with the claimant.
